summary:
Qin Shi Huang (259–210 BC) — 'First Emperor of Qin' — was the most transformative ruler in Chinese history, the man who ended the [[event:1111|Warring States Period]] by conquering all six rival states between 230 and 221 BC and unified China for the first time under a single centralized government. Born Ying Zheng, King of Qin, he chose the new title Huangdi (August Sovereign) to assert his status as supreme ruler, combining characters from the legendary Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors — and the title 'Emperor' has been used by Chinese rulers ever since.

His reign (221–210 BC) compressed an extraordinary program of standardization and construction into eleven years: the [[event:1125|Qin Standardization of Measures]] unified weights, measures, coinage, and written script across all conquered territories; road and canal networks (including the [[event:1126|Lingqu Canal Construction]]) connected the empire; the [[event:1121|Great Wall of China Qin]] was begun to protect the northern frontier; and the [[event:1120|Terracotta Army]] was constructed to guard his tomb. The [[event:1122|Burning of Books and Burying Scholars]] suppressed intellectual opposition to his [[event:1113|Legalism in China]] ideology.

Qin Shi Huang was obsessed with immortality — sending expeditions to find elixirs of eternal life, consuming mercury tonics that may have hastened his death, and constructing an immense mausoleum complex meant to replicate his empire in the afterlife. His death triggered the rapid [[event:1123|Fall of Qin Dynasty]] he had tried to prevent: his chosen successors were incompetent, his chief minister Li Si and eunuch Zhao Gao schemed for power, and mass rebellions broke out within years. Yet his fundamental administrative innovations — prefectural-county bureaucratic structure, standardized script, centralized law — became the lasting template for all subsequent Chinese imperial governance.
